citation.title: Performance comparison of flat sheet and hollow fibre air gap membrane distillation: A mathematical and simulation modelling approach

content: A B S T R A C T Membrane Distillation (MD) systems could address current challenges related to global water demand. AmongMD systems, Air Gap Membrane Distillation (AGMD) systems play crucial roles in addressing this challenge.AGMD systems can be categorized into two main structural groups: flat sheet and hollow fibre forms. It isimportant to study, analyse, model and compare these two systems under the same operational conditions. In thisstudy, a detailed mathematical model of flat sheet and hollow fibre AGMD modules under the same conditions (i.e., membrane type, membrane area, porosity, and pore radius of membrane) has been introduced. The simulationof system performance parameters, including flux, Temperature Polarization Coefficient (TPC), Specific ThermalEnergy Consumption (STEC) and Gain Output Ratio (GOR) have also been conducted, based on various operational conditions and membrane properties. A comprehensive comparison has been then performed between flatsheet AGMD and hollow fibre AGMD systems in terms of flux, TPC, STEC and GOR. The results show thatamongst all operational conditions and membrane properties, feed water temperature, air gap thickness, feedwater Reynolds number, membrane porosity and pore radius significantly impact the flux of both systems.Furthermore, the operational conditions and membrane properties affected the STEC and GOR.

content: 1. Introduction Freshwater shortage is one of the most crucial and comprehensivehuman problems, underscoring the need for sustainable water management and responsible fossil fuel use to reduce the environmentalimpact of energy-intensive water treatment processes [1–6]. Out ofvarious methods that are available, desalination systems could play asignificant role in addressing this issue [7,8]. In further consideringvarious types of distillation systems, including multi-stage desalination,multi-effect desalination, electrodialysis and reverse osmosis, membrane distillation systems have attracted significant attention [9–11].The primary reasons for the prominence of these systems relates totheir capability to desalinate at lower temperatures, thereby lowering heat requirements for operation, as well as their overly uncomplicateddesigns [12,13]. These systems generally can be configured on the difference of fluid in the permeate side and can be categorized into fivemain groups: Direct Contact Membrane Distillation (DCMD), Air GapMembrane Distillation (AGMD), Sweeping Gas Membrane Distillation(SGMD), Water Gap Membrane Distillation (WGMD) and VacuumMembrane Distillation (VMD) [10,14]. In addition to these, there areother types of MD systems, such as Permeate Gap Distillation systems(PGMD) [14]. Additionally, MD systems can be categorized into somegroups based on the structure of the module, flat sheet and hollow fibretypes are two main structure MD systems [15].

citation.title: Performance comparison of flat sheet and hollow fibre air gap membrane distillation: A mathematical and simulation modelling approach

content: tems can be categorized into somegroups based on the structure of the module, flat sheet and hollow fibretypes are two main structure MD systems [15]. Flat sheets consist of asingle thin sheet with a dense barrier layer. In this configuration, thefeedwater flows along one side of the flat sheet membrane, while pressure is applied to drive water molecules through the membrane [16].Furthermore, hollow fibre type MD systems are configured as smallhollow tubes with a hollow centre, whereby the feedwater flows throughthe interior of the hollow fibres while vapor pressure difference causethe water molecules vaporise and move across through the walls of thefibres [17].One of the main categories of MD systems is AGMD systems. In anAGMD configuration, the temperature differential across the hydrophobic membrane induces a partial pressure difference, promptingwater molecules to evaporate at the hot feed side and permeate throughmembrane pores. The vaporized water additionally traverses through astagnant air gap positioned between the membrane and a condensationplate, where it condenses at a lower temperature, yielding distilledwater [18,19]. Due to its high thermal efficiency and low electricitydemand, AGMD has recently attracted widespread attention [20,21].Furthermore, AGMD exhibits a decreased permeate flux due to theadded resistance to mass transfer [22].

citation.title: Performance comparison of flat sheet and hollow fibre air gap membrane distillation: A mathematical and simulation modelling approach

content: recently attracted widespread attention [20,21].Furthermore, AGMD exhibits a decreased permeate flux due to theadded resistance to mass transfer [22]. However, similar to other MDconfigurations, AGMD systems suffer from Temperature Polarization(TP), which is typically quantified via a dimensionless index known asthe Temperature Polarization Coefficient (TPC). Therefore, calculatingTPC in these systems is essential. As the TPC value trends closer to 1, thisindicates better performance, suggesting minimal temperature polarization and more efficient operation [23–25]. TP refers to a situationwhere the temperature of the bulk feed solution differs from the temperature at the interface of the membrane [26]. TP results in a significant decrease in water vapor mass transfer, typically ranging from 50 %to 80 %, which consequently leads to lower distillate flux [27]. Theprimary performance indicators for AGMD systems, which are crucialfor assessing efficiency, thermal distribution, and productivity, encompass permeate flux, TPC, gain output ratio (GOR) and specific thermalenergy consumption (STEC) [28–30].In order to investigate the impact of operational conditions onpermeate flux, TPC, GOR and STEC on flat sheet AGMD systems, Xu et al.have examined the impact of operational conditions including feedtemperatures and flow velocities, as well as membrane characteristicslike material, thickness and pore size, on permeate flux of a flat sheetAGMD.

They observed that increasing feed temperature enhancedpermeate flux, while feed flow velocity had a minimal effect. Moreover,smaller pore sizes resulted in reduced flux, and increased membranethickness led to a decrease in permeate flux [31]. Ali et al. have reportedthat increasing porosity from 60 % to 90 % resulted in a rise in mass fluxand TPC values, from 5 g m-2 s to 13 g m-2 s and 0.7 to 0.85, respectively. As the feed flow rate temperature increased from 50 ◦C to 75 ◦C,the mass flux in their study increased from 1 g m-2 s to 5 g m-2 s in a flatsheet AGMD module [32]. Ansari et al. have investigated the impact ofoperational conditions, in particular via solar absorbers, on TPC and fluxof a flat sheet AGMD system via computational fluid dynamics modelling. Their findings indicated that solar absorbers could slightly enhancewater flux while reducing both temperature and concentration polarization effects. Their results were shown to be more sensitive to changesin air-gap thickness compared to the use of solar absorbers [24]. Afterexamining previous research, it is evident that the critical parametersinfluencing the TPC and performance of both flat sheet and hollow fibreAGMD systems can be categorized into two main groups including:operational conditions (e.g., feed water temperature, feed water flowrate or velocity, coolant water temperature, coolant water flow rate orvelocity, air gap thickness and salt concentration) and membraneproperties (e.g., membrane area, porosity, thickness and membrane poresize).To the best of the authors’ knowledge, there is a lack of detailedanalysis regarding the effects of all operational conditions and materialproperties on system performance indicators, in particular, focusing onflux, TPC, GOR and STEC for both flat sheet and hollow fibre AGMDsystems. Even though studies have been conducted to investigate theeffects of operational conditions on performance indicators, these havenot covered all the operational parameters and material properties. Todate, detailed investigations have not accounted for the effects of alloperational parameters and material properties on TP.

Furthermore,there is a lack of direct comparison between flat sheet AGMD and hollowfibre AGMD systems under similar conditions, scales and materials,which needs to be understood for better decision-making regardingsystem structure.To address these concerns, this research aims to conduct a comprehensive comparative modelling analysis, simulation and comparison offlat sheet and hollow fibre AGMD systems under the same operationalconditions and material properties. To develop mathematical and simulation models of both flat sheetand hollow fibre AGMD systems while considering the impact of alloperational conditions on TP, including feed water temperature, feedwater flow rate, coolant water temperature, coolant water flow rate,air gap thickness, feed water Reynolds number, salt concentration,and membrane properties such as porosity, thickness, and pore sizein same membrane area. Systems description 2.1. Flat sheet air gap membrane distillation (AGMD) Fig. 1 shows the schematic of the flat sheet AGMD module. The feedwater flows through the feed water channel, which is separated from theair gap layer by the membrane surface. In this process, the evaporatedwater can pass through the membrane pores and enter the air gap layer.Directly below this, there is a condensate plate in the air gap layer that facilitates the condensation of distilled water. This plate is cooled by thecold-water flow in the cooling section.

Hollow fibre air gap membrane distillation (AGMD) A schematic of the hollow fibre AGMD module is shown in Fig. 2.Similar to the flat sheet type, the feed water flows through the fibres,whereby it serves as the feed water channel. The outer surface of thefibres is covered in a membrane that separates the feed water channelfrom the air gap layer. The air gap layer is covered with a condensationsurface. Furthermore, cold water flows around the fibre space to providethe cooling energy required for condensing the evaporated water. Methodology In both flat sheet and hollow fibre AGMD modules, operations havebeen maintained under stable conditions while disregarding transientphenomena. Given that the air gap is relatively narrow in comparison tothe overall AGMD structure, the air within this gap is assumed to remainstatic. The flow within the membrane channels is characterized aslaminar, as the Reynolds number addressed in this study remainedbelow 2300. If Re exceeds 2300, turbulent flow considerations wouldnecessitate a revision of the Nusselt number formula. The followingassumptions have been considered in the AGMD model. (1) Heat transfer within the AGMD is one-dimensional and steadystate [46];(2) The air within the narrow air gap is considered stationary [46, 47].(3) The fluid flow within the membrane channels in both systems isincompressible and laminar [46,48].(4) Concentration polarization in the feed channel of both systems isneglected [46,49].

The Fickian model,Stefan-Maxwell Equations [39,61–63] and the dusty-gas model havebeen widely used for modelling mass transfer in porous membranes. Inthe MD process, the mass transfer across the membrane is typicallydescribed by the dusty gas model [39,64,65]. This model includesKnudsen diffusion, molecular diffusion, and their combination, knownas Poiseuille flow. In a conventional AGMD process, the total pressure isapproximately 1 atm, and the operating temperature is below 100 ◦C.Therefore, Poiseuille flow can be disregarded [39]. The Knudsen number is defined as the ratio of the mean free path of the transportedmolecules to the diameter of the membrane pores [39,66]. Kn = λr(30) where, he λ and r are the mean molecular free path and average poreradius of the membrane, respectively. In a binary mixture of air andwater vapor, the mean free path (λ) of the vapor molecules in air can becalculated based on the average membrane temperature (Tm) [39,67].

content: The comparison of pressure drops, feed pump power, and heattransfer enhancement to friction loss ratio(RHT/FL )of flat sheet andhollow fibre systems for various feedwater flowrates is presented inFig. 8. Fig. 8(a) confirms that increasing the feedwater flow rate, asdescribed in Equations 19 and 44, led to a rise in pressure drop in bothflat sheet and hollow fibre systems. Similarly, as shown in Fig. 8(b), thisincrease in pressure drop results in higher pump power consumption inboth systems, as predicted by Equation 43. Furthermore, Fig. 8 illustrates that, based on Equations 43 and 44, the hollow fibre system,having a lower hydraulic diameter than the flat-sheet system, experiences higher pressure drops and greater pump power consumption.Specifically, in the hollow fibre system, at a feedwater flow rate of 100 lh-1, the pressure drop, and pump power consumption are 583 Pa and0.02 kWh.m-3, respectively, while at 250 l h-1, these values increase to1460 Pa and 0.12 kWh.m-3 respectively. In contrast, for the flat-sheetsystem, the pressure drop, and pump power at 100 l.h-1are 127 Paand 0.004 kWh.m-3, respectively, and at 250 l h-1, they rise to 319 Paand 0.027 kWh.m-3, respectively. This comparison highlights that froman economic and energy efficiency perspective, the flat-sheet system ismore advantageous due to its significantly lower pumping power requirements compared to the hollow fibre system.

At low flow rates, Rinitially increases due to significant heat transfer improvement. As theflow rate reaches moderate levels, R peaks as the rate of heat transfergain slows while friction losses continue to rise. At high flow rates, Rdeclinessharplyduetotherapidincreaseinfrictionallosses outweighing heat transfer improvements. Additionally, the flat sheetsystem consistently exhibits a higher R value compared to the hollowfibre system across all feedwater flow rates. For instance, at a feedwaterflow rate of 100 l h-1, R is 2.55 for the flat-sheet system and 1.34 for thehollow fibre system. As the flow rate increases to 250 l h-1, R decreasesto 1.38 and 0.72, respectively, corresponding to a reduction of 54 % and53 % for the flat sheet and hollow fibre systems, respectively. This trendhighlights the impact of increasing feedwater flow rate on the trade-offbetween heat transfer efficiency and frictional losses in both AGMDconfigurations.

The study hascomprehensively compared these systems in terms of flux, TPC, STECand GOR, based on operating conditions and membrane properties inboth laminar and turbulent feed water flow conditions. The findings canbe summarized as follows. The hollow fibre AGMD system has higher flux, TPC and GOR andlower STEC than the flat sheet AGMD system in all operationalconditions and membrane properties (Figs. 6–15). For instance, inFig. 6, the flux value for hollow fibre and flat sheet AGMD systems atsimulation conditions and 80 ◦C feed water temperature is approximately 2.1 l m-2 h-1 and 1.4 l m-2 h-1, respectively. Additionally, inFig. 7, the TPC value for hollow fibre and flat sheet AGMD systems atsimulation conditions and 250 l h-1 feed water flow rate is approximately 0.86 and 0.6, respectively. The comparison of pressure drops and feed pump power between flatsheet and hollow fibre systems (Fig. 8) indicates that increasing thefeedwater flow rate leads to higher pressure drops and pump powerconsumption in both systems.

However, due to its lower hydraulicdiameter, the hollow fibre system experiences significantly greaterpressure drops (up to 1460 Pa) and higher pump power consumption(up to 0.12 kWh.m-3) compared to the flat sheet system (319 Pa,0.027 kWh.m-3 at the same flow rate), making the flat-sheet systemmore energy efficient and economically favourable. In addition to, asthe feedwater flow rate increases, the heat transfer enhancement tofriction loss ratio (R) decreases in both flat sheet and hollow fibreAGMD systems. This decline is attributed to the rapid increase in frictional losses surpassing the gains in heat transfer efficiency.Notably, the flat sheet system consistently exhibits higher R valuesacross all tested flow rates compared to the hollow fibre system. Forexample, at a feedwater flow rate of 100 l h-1, the R value is 2.55 forthe flat-sheet system and 1.34 for the hollow fibre system. As the flowrate increases to 250 l h-1, R decreases to 1.38 and 0.72, respectively, representing a reduction of 54 % and 53 % for the flat-sheetand hollow fibre systems. This trend emphasizes how increasingthe feedwater flow rate affects the balance between heat transferefficiency and frictional losses in both AGMD configurations.
